items.front: What does Table 1-1-1's cutoff date represent?
items.back: The deadline by which a proposed change must be fully coordinated and signed to make the associated publication effective date.

items.front: What is the operational purpose of the flight-strip hand-printing figure?
items.back: To prevent misinterpretation by standardizing how hand-printed strip characters are written.

items.front: When may a horizontal line be drawn through an altitude being vacated on a strip?
items.back: Only after the aircraft has reported or is observed by valid Mode C leaving that altitude.

items.front: What is the key lesson from the taxi-route figure in 3-7-2?
items.back: Taxi and vehicle clearances must include the specific movement-area route; runway crossing and hold-short points must be explicit.

items.front: What do the same-runway departure-separation figures emphasize?
items.back: A succeeding aircraft cannot begin takeoff roll until the preceding aircraft has crossed the runway end, turned to avert conflict, or is airborne with the required landmark-based distance.

items.front: How do the same-runway arrival-separation figures use threshold distance?
items.back: During daylight with suitable landmarks, a landed aircraft need not be clear if the applicable distance from the landing threshold exists before the next arrival crosses the threshold.

items.front: What is the point of the 4-1-1 altitude/distance figures?
items.back: They show how to apply NAVAID usable-distance and altitude limits to each controlled-airspace segment of a non-airway route.

items.front: What do the approach-clearance figures in 4-8-1 protect against?
items.back: Clearing an aircraft for an approach before it is properly positioned to begin at an IAF or IF, established on a published segment, or otherwise meeting the direct-to/intercept conditions.

items.front: For Table 5-5-2 wake turbulence on approach, when must the listed spacing exist?
items.back: At the time the preceding aircraft is over the landing threshold.

items.front: Under Table 5-5-2, what spacing applies to a Category H follower behind a Category C leader on approach?
What maximum final approach course intercept angle applies less than 2 miles from the approach gate or during triple simultaneous approaches?
What maximum final approach course intercept angle normally applies 2 miles or more from the approach gate?
items.back: 30 degrees, with 45 degrees allowed for helicopters.

items.front: At what point may the diagonal minimum be used for simultaneous dependent approaches?
items.back: After aircraft are established on adjacent final approach courses; turn-on still requires 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.

items.front: If a diverging-radials angle falls between two table values in 6-5-2, which value is used?
items.back: Use the lesser divergence value to obtain the required distance.

items.front: In the 6-5-2 non-DME divergence table, what distance applies at 30 degrees?
For same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN track separation, what else is required besides at least 15 degrees of divergence?
items.back: One aircraft must be clear of the protected airspace for the other aircraft, using the table distance for the divergence and altitude band.

items.front: In Table 8-4-1, what distance applies to VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks diverging 26 to 35 degrees at FL230 and below?
How should domestic CPDLC message tables be used relative to urgent voice clearances?
items.back: They define the approved CPDLC message set, but immediate or expeditious clearances should not use CPDLC unless voice is not operationally feasible.

items.front: Why does oceanic CPDLC guidance prefer the message set over free text?
items.back: The message set supports proper closure of CPDLC exchanges; free text should be avoided to the extent possible.
